title: Quantum Hall Phases, plasma analogy and incompressibility estimates
speaker: Jakob Yngvason
abstract: When a 2D many-particle system with a repulsive interaction is subject to a sufficiently strong magnetic field, that can also be produced by rapid rotation, strongly correlated many-body states in the lowest Landau level LLL may emerge. In the talk conditions for the ground state to include the Laughlin state as a fact or will be presented, together with an analysis of the particle density in such states. This is joint work with Sylvia Serfaty and Nicolas Rougerie.

proof_stages.label: Laughlin-class LLL variational reduction
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines Laughlin-type lowest-Landau-level wave functions with analytic symmetric correlation factors, notes that they annihilate the contact interaction, and reduces the restricted ground-state problem to understanding which one-particle densities can occur and how incompressible they are.

proof_stages.label: Rotating trap and angular-momentum spectral framework
proof_stages.summary: For the quadratic-plus-quartic rotating Bose-gas trap, the physical potential is related to angular-momentum operators, leading to the Eyrast-curve picture, the exact Laughlin state at special parameters, and a gap conjecture used later to formulate simple strong-correlation criteria.

proof_stages.label: Plasma analogy for quasi-hole and giant-vortex trial states
proof_stages.summary: The trial states obtained by multiplying the Laughlin state by powers of the particle coordinates are scaled and rewritten as Gibbs measures of a two-dimensional Coulomb gas at temperature of order 1/N, then compared with a mean-field free-energy minimizer by electrostatic and entropy estimates.

proof_stages.label: Optimized mean-field profiles and phase criteria
proof_stages.summary: Solving the zero-temperature mean-field Euler-Lagrange equation gives saturated disk/annulus profiles and, for large holes, Gaussian-like profiles; optimizing over the hole strength and comparing with lower bounds yields sufficient conditions for Laughlin, annular, and giant-vortex correlated phases.

proof_stages.label: Need for general incompressibility lower bounds
proof_stages.summary: The trial-state phase crossings are identified as non-sharp without a lower bound for all fully correlated states, motivating a general formulation in terms of marginal factorization and a bathtub-energy consequence of incompressibility.

proof_stages.label: Finite de Finetti comparison for special correlation factors
proof_stages.summary: For product-form analytic correlation factors, the many-body free energy is compared with a polynomial-dependent mean-field functional, and a quantitative Diaconis-Freedman finite de Finetti theorem supplies the approximation needed to prove bathtub/incompressibility bounds in this restricted class.

proof_stages.label: Conjectural irrelevance of extra correlations for energy lowering
proof_stages.summary: The talk ends with the conjecture that additional correlations beyond those already present in the Laughlin state may adapt the density to a potential but cannot lower the optimal energy.

examples.name: N=400 Laughlin-density edge-peak plot
examples.note: The numerical density profile shows that even Laughlin-type states can have edge peaks, illustrating why the intended incompressibility bound must be weak rather than pointwise.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker reads the N=400 density plot, identifies the different filling factors and the Laughlin edge peak, and uses it to argue that a pointwise incompressibility bound cannot hold.

passes.reason: The finite pure Laughlin droplet is radially regular and bulk-densest, while its edge peak is used to rule out a literal pointwise density bound.
the finite-N Laughlin density plot is used to show that a pointwise incompressibility bound fails because of the edge peak
the finite-N radial Laughlin density plot is used to show a pointwise incompressibility bound fails because of the edge peak
The finite pure Laughlin droplet is a maximally regular/densest zero-mode, and its edge peak is used to rule out a pointwise incompressibility bound.
The finite-N Laughlin droplet density is rotationally symmetric, and its edge peak is used to show a pointwise incompressibility bound cannot hold.
The finite-N Laughlin droplet is rotationally invariant and maximal-filling among zero-contact states, and its edge peak is used to show a strict pointwise density bound fails.
The finite Laughlin droplet is a maximally filled rotationally invariant zero-mode, and its edge overshoot is used to refute a literal pointwise incompressibility bound.
The finite radially symmetric Laughlin droplet, a maximum-density zero-mode, is used to show a strict pointwise density cap fails at the edge peak.
Finite-N Laughlin density is used to show a pointwise incompressibility bound fails at the edge, while the pure Laughlin droplet is the maximally dense zero-mode and is rotationally regular.

examples.name: Negative quartic plus sixth-order confinement

examples.engagement_reason: the negative quartic plus sixth-order confinement is only cited as a type of potential where the angular-momentum shortcut would not work.

examples.name: Small-N Eyrast-curve spectrum picture
examples.note: The adapted numerical joint-spectrum plot illustrates daughter states, the Laughlin point, spectral gaps, and later the slope construction for choosing a ground state.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker reasons through the Eyrast spectrum picture, explaining the joint spectrum, daughter states, gaps, and how a slope line selects the ground state.

passes.reason: An yrast curve is by definition the lower-envelope, minimal-energy boundary of the joint spectrum.

examples.name: Emperor-penguin packing analogy
examples.note: The penguin-colony picture visualizes incompressible packing and the collective outward motion caused by a central repulsion, paralleling the droplet-to-annulus transition.
examples.engagement_reason: the penguin-packing analogy is developed step by step to model tight packing, correlated motion, a repulsive central hole, and the eventual lower-density regime.

passes.reason: The idealized round close packing of penguins represents a maximal-density/minimal-surface packing analogy.

examples.name: Non-rotational potential requiring a complicated factor
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker notes that a non-radial potential would require a more complicated analytic factor, illustrating the conjectural role of extra correlations as shape-adapting rather than energy-lowering.
examples.engagement_reason: the non-rotational potential is only invoked hypothetically to say one would need a more complicated factor than a power of z.

passes.reason: A generic non-rotational trap is invoked to show that a simple radial power factor cannot suffice for arbitrary potentials.
